metadata.match_reason: JD explicitly lists LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, LangGraph, RAG, tool calling, multi-agent - near-perfect overlap with Amit's RAG/LLM/FastAPI/production AI stack. India's sovereign LLM flagship.
metadata.cold_outreach_angle: Lead with ARYA team fit: production RAG at 50K queries/month + LangGraph agentic upgrade + FastAPI multi-tenant infra. Reference Arya blog's immutable ledger design and how Amit's permission-aware RAG architecture mirrors their boundary-scoped state model.
metadata.notes: Govt-selected for India sovereign LLM; $53M Series A. Email pattern confirmed: firstname@sarvam.ai (from Karman Sethi's public profile). 38 open roles. ARYA team is the best fit: MCP servers, LangGraph, multi-agent, Kafka pipelines. Samvaad (voice/conversational AI) is secondary fit.

research_summary: Sarvam AI is India's govt-selected sovereign LLM company ($53M Series A, Lightspeed/Peak XV/Khosla). The FDSE role spans 4 product teams; ARYA is the primary fit - it's their agent orchestration stack built on 8 composable primitives, immutable state ledger, and declarative HCL authoring. Amit's LangGraph multi-agent RAG chatbot (50K queries/month, 250+ permissions, multi-tenant) maps directly to what ARYA is solving at production scale. Email pattern confirmed as firstname@sarvam.ai. Key contacts: Aalekh Sharan (Head Agentic AI, Tier 1), Vivek Raghavan + Pratyush Kumar (Co-Founders, Tier 2), 3 FDSEs on the same team (Tier 3). Recent hooks: Aalekh on Tech-à-Tête podcast April 2026; Open-source 30B/105B March 2026; Arya blog Feb 2026.
jd_full_text:
ENGINEERING
Forward Deployed Software Engineer | 2-5 years
Engineering | Bengaluru | Full Time | On-Site

About Sarvam
Sarvam is building the bedrock of Sovereign AI for India. The company is developing India's full-stack sovereign AI platform, building across research, models, infrastructure and applications with a singular focus on making AI genuinely work for India. Sarvam works with leading enterprises and public institutions and is backed by Lightspeed, Peak XV, and Khosla Ventures. Sarvam partners with India's leading brands, including Tata Capital, SBI Life, CRED, IDFC, and LIC.

About the Role
We are looking for high-agency engineers who can architect and build production-grade solutions using generative AI and agentic systems. As an FDSE at Sarvam, you will work directly with customers and product teams, understand their problems deeply, and ship real solutions fast - not prototypes or demos, but things that run in production.

This is not a single opening. We have multiple positions across Samvaad, API Dashboard, Content Studio, and ARYA - and we will match you to the team where your skills and interests fit best.

What You'll Do

Samvaad: Build, deploy, monitor, and evaluate conversational AI agents across channels - Voice, WhatsApp, website (voice and chat), and in-app - integrating them deeply into client systems and workflows. Scope and design end-to-end technical solutions for client requirements. Own client engagements from kickoff to go-live. Instrument and improve deployed agents: debug failure modes, tune prompts, and iterate on agent behaviour based on real usage data.

API Dashboard: Build plugins, integrations, and developer-facing tooling. Contribute to open-source projects. Own the developer experience end-to-end.

Content Studio: Own end-to-end deployment of Sarvam's dubbing and live translation APIs into client and partner production environments. Serve as primary technical point of contact with clients.

ARYA: Build MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers and agentic backend infrastructure. Apply strong system design thinking to architect multi-agent systems: tool calling, memory, orchestration, and inter-agent coordination. Design and maintain data pipelines using messaging systems like Kafka, SQS, or RabbitMQ. Work with SQL and NoSQL databases at scale. Work with agentic frameworks like LangGraph and Google ADK. Contribute to internal tooling and infrastructure.

What We're Looking For
- 2-5 years of software engineering experience with a track record of building and shipping production systems
- Strong Python fluency and comfort across the stack
- Deep understanding of how LLMs and agents work - context windows, tokens, embeddings, RAG, tool calling, prompt design, and failure modes; hands-on experience with both open and closed-source LLMs
- Experience designing and implementing multi-agent architectures, including memory management and orchestration
- Ability to set up eval pipelines and define meaningful quality metrics for agentic AI systems
- Systems thinking: ability to reason about architecture, reliability, scale, trade-offs, and failure modes
- High agency - you unblock yourself, figure things out, and ship without hand-holding

Bonus Points
- End-to-end deployment of working AI agents in production
- Side projects, open-source contributions, or products shipped outside of work
- Prior experience in a customer-facing or product-embedded engineering role
- Deep familiarity with LLM orchestration frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, LangGraph)
- Experience with voice/speech models (TTS, STT, ASR), cloud platforms, or real-time media and streaming pipelines
- Familiarity with Sarvam models and APIs from personal or project use
tailoring_notes: 1. Summary rewritten to lead with LangGraph + multi-agent (JD: 'multi-agent architectures, memory management, orchestration') and MRR numbers; removed 'edge ML' entirely (not relevant for ARYA/FDSE). 2. Skills reordered: Programming Languages first (JD: 'strong Python fluency'); AI/ML Frameworks second with LangGraph added explicitly (JD bonus: 'LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, LangGraph'); new 'Agentic AI and RAG' line added for tool calling + eval design (JD requirements); Hardware/IoT line dropped entirely (not relevant). 3. Vivansh bullet 1: LangGraph made explicit as 'stateful multi-agent graph' (mirrors JD ARYA team description). 4. IIT Delhi work experience removed (hardware/wearable research not relevant for pure software AI role; kept 1000+ model eval credibility in edge). 5. Projects section (Voicer + WeepScope) removed; Samsung Top 10 moved to Certifications as one-liner. 6. Margins tightened to 0.9cm top/bottom to achieve one-page fit.

contacts.contact_role: Head, Agentic AI & Head, Public Sector
contacts.research_hook: Appeared on Tech-à-Tête podcast Episode 2 (~April 2026) discussing building AI that is safe, sovereign, and scalable - specifically multilingual model challenges and compute constraints for production agentic systems. Also spoke at TEDxBITS (The Odyssey). As Head of Agentic AI, directly leads the ARYA team that Amit is best suited for.
contacts.subject: question about ARYA's production agent stack
contacts.content:
Aalekh,

Listened to your Tech-à-Tête episode last month. The bit about compute constraints forcing you to rethink how agents actually behave under production load was what made me go deep on the ARYA blog post after.

Quick context on why I'm writing: I spent the last year upgrading an EHS chatbot to a LangGraph stateful multi-agent architecture: 250+ permission checks per query, 500+ concurrent users, Milvus backend. Before that, a FAISS to Milvus migration brought MRR from 45% to 95% after benchmarking 6 embedding models on production data. The constraint-as-design-principle framing in the ARYA post maps pretty directly to what we hit trying to make permission-aware retrieval reliable at scale.

I'm applying for the FDSE role on the ARYA team. If you have 15 minutes next Tuesday or Wednesday, I'd like to hear how the ARYA primitives hold up under multi-tenant load.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: question about ARYA's production agent stack
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Aalekh,

Following up in case this got buried. Since I wrote last week, put up a short write-up on amitgohel.dev on the permission-aware RAG architecture; the part about separating the permission index from the content index is what I think maps closest to ARYA's ledger scoping. Happy to share it if useful.

Amit
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: question about ARYA's production agent stack
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Aalekh,

Last note on this. Saw Sarvam's ASR evaluation post from April. The LLM-WER and intent scoring framing reminded me of the eval pipelines we built for the agentic chatbot. If the FDSE role or timing works better later, I'd still appreciate being on your radar.

Amit

contacts.contact_role: Head of Edge AI & GTM Leadership
contacts.research_hook: Leads Edge AI and GTM - relevant to Samvaad/API Dashboard teams. Sarvam Edge launched Feb 14, 2026. ex-McKinsey + Google AI4SG background; IIT Kanpur.
contacts.subject: Sarvam Edge, ARYA, and the FDSE opening
contacts.content:
Tushar,

Saw the Sarvam Edge announcement in February. On-device inference at scale with the constraint of Indian hardware budgets is a genuinely different problem than deploying a quantized model and calling it done.

Context: I'm applying for the FDSE role at Sarvam. The last two years have been production RAG infrastructure: FAISS to Milvus migration (MRR 45% to 95%), LangGraph multi-agent upgrade handling 250+ permission checks per query, multi-model orchestration across GPT-4o, Gemini, and Qwen3. The API Dashboard and ARYA tracks on the FDSE spec match the kind of work I've been doing.

Would you be open to 15 minutes this week? Wanted to hear how the edge and cloud inference tracks at Sarvam divide the problem space.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: Sarvam Edge, ARYA, and the FDSE opening
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Tushar,

Following up on this. Since I wrote last week, dug deeper into the ARYA team description in the FDSE posting; the MCP server and Kafka pipeline work is what I'd lean into most. If there's a good time to connect, I'm free most mornings.

Amit
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: Sarvam Edge, ARYA, and the FDSE opening
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Tushar,

Last note. If now's not the right time, no worries. I'll keep watching what Sarvam ships. Good luck with the Edge rollout.

Amit

contacts.research_hook: Co-founded Sarvam with mission 'GENAI for a Billion Indians.' Open-sourced Sarvam 30B and 105B on March 6, 2026 - India's largest open-source LLM release. The scale of production inference challenges at 30B/105B maps to Amit's multi-model orchestration work.
contacts.subject: thoughts on the 30B open-source release
contacts.content:
Vivek,

Followed the 30B and 105B open-source release in March, specifically the production inference choices at that scale. Serving 30B at reasonable latency for Indian enterprise loads is a different problem than serving 7B, and the decision to release both weights and checkpoints was notable.

Context: I spent the last year at Vivansh rebuilding RAG infrastructure serving 50K+ queries/month: FAISS to Milvus migration brought MRR from 45% to 95%, LangGraph multi-agent upgrade with 250+ permission checks per query. The orchestration layer ended up handling GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5, and Qwen3 in the same pipeline. Working against Sarvam's own models would be a genuinely interesting set of problems.

I'm applying for the FDSE role, ARYA track. If a 20-minute call makes sense, I'm free most mornings next week.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: thoughts on the 30B open-source release
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Vivek,

Following up from last week. Put a short project write-up on amitgohel.dev; the multi-tenant permission-aware RAG section is probably the most relevant to what ARYA is solving at production scale. Happy to walk through it if useful.

Amit
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: thoughts on the 30B open-source release
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Vivek,

Last note from me. If the FDSE timing isn't right or the headcount's full, understood. Would still appreciate being on your radar when it opens up.

Amit

contacts.research_hook: Authored the ARYA technical blog post (Feb 10, 2026) - immutable state ledger, 8 composable primitives, declarative HCL authoring, Acta Diurna tracing. The constraint 'same model + same task, one approach collapsed under context, the other finished - difference wasn't intelligence, it was structure' maps directly to Amit's production RAG optimization work.
contacts.subject: question about ARYA's immutable state ledger
contacts.content:
Pratyush,

Read the ARYA post when it came out in February and came back to it a couple times since. The observation about context collapse: same model, same task, two approaches, only one survives production. That's a failure mode I've hit and didn't have a clean framework for until your post.

Background: I've been building production RAG and agentic systems for two years. At Vivansh, upgrading to a LangGraph stateful multi-agent architecture with 250+ permission checks per query was specifically about the structure you describe. Before that, the FAISS to Milvus migration was driven by the same idea: retrieval reliability isn't a model problem, it's a pipeline design problem.

I'm applying for the FDSE role on the ARYA team. Would you have 15 minutes to talk through how the ledger model holds up when task graphs get deeply nested?

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: question about ARYA's immutable state ledger
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Pratyush,

Following up on this. Re-read the HCL declarative authoring section of the ARYA post since I wrote last week. The spec-separate-from-execution model is close to what I ended up building ad hoc with LangGraph's state schema and tool configs. Would be interesting to see how the structured version handles schema evolution in production. Happy to connect if the timing works.

Amit
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: question about ARYA's immutable state ledger
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Pratyush,

Last note. If now's not the right time, no worries. I'll keep watching what ARYA ships.

Amit

Came across the Sovereign AI Partnerships announcement from February and then went back to read about Samta; the thread from access to opportunities there to making government AI actually work for citizens at scale is a clear line.

I'm applying for the FDSE role at Sarvam. The ARYA track on the FDSE spec is where my work maps most directly.

Wanted to reach out directly rather than going through the careers portal. If a 20-minute call makes sense sometime next week, I'd appreciate it.

Amit

contacts.research_hook: FDSE on the exact team Amit is applying to; previously at Fractal AI. With 4K LinkedIn followers, is active and visible. Peer-level reach-out - 'I'm applying for the FDSE role and wanted to hear what the day-to-day looks like from someone on the team.'
contacts.subject: quick question before I apply for FDSE
contacts.content:
Prajna,

Going through the FDSE job description at Sarvam this week and noticed your background at Fractal AI before joining; that trajectory made me want to reach out directly rather than just applying cold.

I'm 2 years into production AI (RAG at 50K+ queries/month, LangGraph multi-agent stack, FAISS to Milvus migration at 45% to 95% MRR) and Sarvam's ARYA track is what drew me in specifically. Wanted to ask: what does the actual day-to-day look like for an FDSE on that team? And is the work more infrastructure-heavy or customer-facing at the moment?

No hard ask. Just curious what you'd tell someone considering the role.

Amit

contacts.subject: shipping conversational AI agents to production
contacts.content:
Dnyaneshwar,

Saw your posts on conversational AI engineering at Sarvam. The production side of it, not the demos. That's the part I've been working on too.

Background: spent the last year upgrading an EHS agentic chatbot from a basic RAG system to a LangGraph stateful multi-agent architecture: Milvus backend, 250+ permission checks per query, 500+ concurrent users. The failure modes on the agentic side (context drift, tool call failures mid-chain) were the interesting problems.

I'm applying for the FDSE role. Wanted to ask: what's the split between building new agent capabilities vs. keeping existing ones reliable in production?

Amit

If you have 15 minutes to talk through what the engineering culture looks like from an IC perspective, I'd appreciate it.
